narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and I know how disappointing it must feel to receive this rejection—we're here to help you understand why it happened in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students to predict if someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 63.8% chance that you might not, which tipped the scales against your application. Your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.60 and LSAT score of 39—both above average for applicants—were positive signs that pulled the prediction somewhat toward success, as did your Year 3 school decile ranking of 6, which beat the typical student's mark. However, your family's middle-income bracket stood out as the biggest concern, pushing the model strongly toward predicting bar failure because students from similar backgrounds have historically faced steeper odds in passing on the first attempt. Your Year 1 decile of 5, a bit below average, added to that worry by suggesting an early academic dip compared to peers. Even your plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students, didn't shift things much in your favor. Overall, while your standout test scores and later grades show real promise, the model weighed your family income and early-year performance as the top risks when stacked against typical applicants who often come from higher-income homes or stronger starting deciles. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how past data shaped the decision for cases like yours. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push that score even higher or bolstering your profile with internships that highlight your resilience as a Black woman in this field. You might also explore schools with holistic reviews that value your unique background more deeply. Keep advocating for yourself; many have turned similar predictions around with targeted improvements. We're rooting for you to ace that bar exam and make your mark in law.
